topic: trying to cook Japanese curry from scratch

turns.content: ok so i decided today was the day i was going to make japanese curry from scratch and i think i've already made a mistake
Oh no — what did you do and how bad is it? Japanese curry is actually pretty forgiving so there's a decent chance it's recoverable. What stage are you at?

i'm at the "staring at my cutting board with potatoes and carrots" stage. the mistake was thinking i could make my own roux instead of using the S&B box like a normal person. i watched one youtube video and thought i was a chef apparently

Okay that's actually a totally recoverable mistake — you haven't done anything yet, you've just made an ambitious choice. Homemade Japanese curry roux is doable, it just requires a few specific things. Are you going for a vegetarian version or with protein? And do you have whole spices on hand or mostly pre-ground stuff?
i don't eat meat so i was going to do like... shrimp? i have garam masala, cumin, turmeric, curry powder, cardamom. also butter, flour, the usual stuff. and i have an onion, garlic, ginger

Shrimp Japanese curry is genuinely delicious and you have a solid spice situation. Here's the thing about Japanese curry roux — it's basically a dark butter-flour roux (like for a béchamel) but you cook it longer until it's nutty and fragrant, then add your spices into the roux itself. The flavor base is what makes it Japanese curry rather than just a generic curry stew.

For your roux: melt about 3 tablespoons of butter over medium-low heat, whisk in 3 tablespoons of flour, and then just keep stirring. You want to cook it for at least 5-7 minutes until it turns a light golden-brown color and smells kind of nutty. Don't rush this part — that's where people go wrong. Then take it off the heat and stir in: 1.5 tbsp curry powder, 1 tsp garam masala, half a tsp turmeric, quarter tsp cumin. Let that bloom in the residual heat for like a minute.

Separately: start your onion in oil until it's very soft and almost jammy — like 15 minutes. That's also non-negotiable for good Japanese curry. What size are you cutting the potatoes and carrots?
i was going to do like... medium chunks? is that right. also 15 minutes for the onion omg i was going to do 5

Medium chunks are perfect — you want them big enough that they don't disintegrate into the sauce, Japanese curry is supposed to have substantial pieces. The classic size is roughly 1.5 to 2 inches, so if that's what you're doing, you're good.

And yes — I know 15 minutes sounds insane but that slow-cooked jammy onion is genuinely the soul of the dish. It gives you sweetness that you can't get any other way. The box roux from S&B works partly because they've already built that sweetness in with sugars and starches. Since you're going from scratch you need to build it yourself. You can add a tiny pinch of sugar if you want to cheat it slightly, but honestly just commit to the onion time. Grate or finely mince your garlic and ginger and add those in the last 2-3 minutes of the onion cooking so they don't burn.
ok ok i'm doing the onion properly. this is already taking longer than i expected lol. also when do the shrimp go in? i feel like they'll get rubbery if i cook them too long

Your instinct is exactly right — shrimp go in last, like literally in the final 3-5 minutes before you serve. The curry sauce should be fully cooked and the potatoes and carrots totally tender before the shrimp ever touch it. Then you just nestle the shrimp into the simmering sauce, put a lid on it, and let them cook through. They'll be done when they're pink and just barely opaque. Pull them the second they're done.

Here's the full order of operations: onion gets jammy, add garlic/ginger, add potatoes and carrots with enough water or stock to just cover (about 2-2.5 cups), simmer until the veg is tender (around 15-20 minutes), then whisk in your roux in chunks and stir until the sauce thickens. Taste and adjust. Then shrimp in at the very end. Serve over Japanese short grain rice if you have it — the starchiness is part of the experience.
i have jasmine rice, will that work? and how much water for the roux i forgot to ask

Jasmine is fine, just not quite as sticky as the short grain but honestly it's good. The roux you already made should be enough to thicken about 2-2.5 cups of liquid — so match your liquid amount to that. When you add the roux to the pot, break it up into smaller pieces and whisk it in a bit at a time rather than dumping it all in at once. The sauce will look thin at first and then all of a sudden thicken up as it simmers — give it about 5 minutes of stirring on medium heat after you add the roux and it should come together.

If it ends up too thick, just splash in a little more water. Too thin, simmer uncovered for a few more minutes. Japanese curry sauce should coat a spoon but still pour — somewhere between a gravy and a thick stew.
ok the onion is ACTUALLY jammy now and i added the garlic and ginger and my apartment smells incredible. i feel like i might actually be doing this

That's the moment — when your apartment smells like that, you've already won. Everything after this is just not messing it up, which is the easy part. Get your potatoes and carrots in with the stock or water now and let them do their thing. How are you feeling about the roux — did it get to that golden-nutty stage or did it stay pretty pale?
it got golden! i was so proud of myself. it smelled kind of like toasted nuts which i think is right? i also added a tiny splash of soy sauce to the base because i read that somewhere

Yes — toasted nuts smell is exactly right, that's the Maillard reaction happening in the roux and it's a very good sign. And soy sauce in Japanese curry is actually completely traditional, a lot of recipes use a tablespoon or so. It adds umami and a little depth without tasting obviously soy-forward. Good instinct.

You're honestly doing really well for someone who "made a mistake" 45 minutes ago. Once those potatoes are tender you're basically there. Are you eating this with anyone or is this a solo sunday dinner situation?
raj is around so we're eating together which is good because i made a truly alarming amount of this. there's no way two people finish this pot

Japanese curry reheats beautifully though — arguably better the next day when everything has had time to meld. The sauce thickens up overnight in the fridge so you might need to add a splash of water when you reheat it, but the flavor gets deeper. You basically accidentally made meal prep.

One last tip: when you add the shrimp, season them lightly with salt and pepper before they go in. And taste the sauce before you serve — if it needs a little brightness, a tiny squeeze of lemon or a small splash of rice vinegar can lift it. Japanese curry can sometimes feel a bit flat if it's all savory with no acid.
ok shrimp are in and they look perfect and the sauce is this gorgeous deep brown color and honestly i'm unreasonably proud of myself rn

You should be! You talked yourself into making a whole roux from scratch on a Monday, slow-cooked your onions properly, got the Maillard reaction right — that's not beginner behavior. Enjoy it. Report back on whether Raj approves.
raj said it's the best thing i've ever cooked and honestly... he might be right. 10/10 would stress-cook again

That's the highest possible endorsement. You've also now unlocked "I can make Japanese curry from scratch" which is genuinely a useful life skill. Save the roux ratios somewhere — next time you can make a big batch of the roux in advance and keep it in the fridge. It lasts weeks and means future-you can have curry in 30 minutes whenever you want.
